Made cheaply, in black and white, with a TV crew, it destroyed several classical conventions simultaneously: the star who dies mid-film, the plot engine (the money) that becomes irrelevant, the identification that transfers to the killer. It proved that Hitchcock’s formal concerns — the manipulation of the gaze, the implication of the spectator — could operate at the mass-entertainment level without the audience recognizing what was being done to them.
Connects to

Architecture as psychology in cinema — the Bates house/motel as the most literal psychic architecture in the vault: mother above, son below, the staircase as the axis of a divided self
Identity as performance — Norman performing the good son while Mother operates beneath; identity fractured not by postmodern play but by psychic splitting
Active spectatorship in modernist cinema — the identification transfer from Marion to Norman forces the audience into moral complicity; Hitchcock weaponizes classical editing grammar
Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism — the shower scene as a demonstration of what editing does: fragments, dismembers, constructs violence from rhythm. Norman’s final stare into the camera breaks the fourth wall.
Feminist frameworks in cinema — Marion punished for transgression (the theft, the sex); the female body dismembered by the camera before the knife
(1958) Vertigo — companion piece: both films study what happens when the male gaze loses its object and tries to replace or recreate it
(1986) Blue Velvet — Lynch’s closet scene descends directly from Hitchcock’s peephole: voyeurism as structural principle, the spectator implicated in looking

FILM/FILMS/(1960)-The-Apartment.title: (1960) The Apartment
FILM/FILMS/(1960)-The-Apartment.content:
Director: Billy Wilder
Movement: Classical Hollywood cinema
C.C. Baxter is a low-level insurance clerk at a massive New York corporation. He’s discovered a shortcut to promotion: lending his apartment to executives for their extramarital affairs. The system works — he advances — until he discovers that one of the executives, personnel director Jeff Sheldrake, is using the apartment to carry on an affair with Fran Kubelik, the elevator operator Baxter is in love with. On Christmas Eve, Fran attempts suicide in Baxter’s apartment. He saves her. He quits. She leaves Sheldrake. They end up together, playing cards on New Year’s Eve.
The Apartment is Wilder’s most formally precise film about how institutions organize private life. The insurance company isn’t a backdrop — it’s the system that produces every relationship in the film: Baxter’s compliance, Sheldrake’s entitlement, Fran’s despair. The apartment itself is the site where corporate power converts into sexual access, and Baxter’s moral education consists entirely of learning to stop handing over the key. Wilder tells this story as a romantic comedy, which is what makes it devastating — the sweetness is real, and so is the ugliness it grows from.
Key formal strategies

The insurance office: deep-focus compositions showing endless rows of identical desks, Baxter lost in the grid
The apartment as contested space: domestic, romantic, corporate, and suicidal by turns
Jack Lemmon’s physical comedy deployed to express institutional humiliation
Shirley MacLaine’s performance: warmth and damage held simultaneously, no protective distance
Classical structure (boy meets girl, complications, resolution) used to tell a story about systemic exploitation


The office
Wilder opens with Baxter’s voiceover delivering insurance statistics — the number of employees, the square footage, his desk number — over a shot of the office floor that seems to extend to infinity. Hundreds of identical desks in geometric rows, each occupied by an identical figure. Baxter is one among thousands, distinguished only by his willingness to be used.
The deep-focus composition isn’t decorative. It’s the film’s thesis visualized: the corporation as architecture that reduces individuals to units, arranged for maximum efficiency, interchangeable. The space doesn’t express Baxter’s interiority — it suppresses it. There is no interiority available in this grid. Personality exists only in the margins: the apartment after hours, the elevator between floors, the bar on the corner. The office is where you perform compliance; the apartment is where the compliance is cashed in.
The apartment
Baxter’s apartment is the film’s structural center — a West Side one-bedroom that is never fully his. Executives use it on rotation. Baxter schedules around their needs, sitting on a park bench in the cold while his bosses conduct affairs in his bed. The apartment is domestic space colonized by corporate hierarchy: the same power relations that govern the office extend into the bedroom, the bathroom, the kitchen.
When Fran takes the sleeping pills there, the apartment becomes something else — a site of crisis that forces Baxter to choose between the system and the person. His decision to care for Fran (the long sequence of walking her, making her soup, keeping her awake) is the first time the apartment has been used for genuine human connection. The space doesn’t change; what changes is who controls it.
Fran
Shirley MacLaine’s Fran Kubelik is the film’s most carefully drawn figure. She operates the elevator — she moves people between floors, between levels of the hierarchy, and goes nowhere herself. She’s smart, she’s warm, she’s funny, and she’s in a relationship with a man who will never leave his wife for her and who uses a borrowed apartment to see her because he doesn’t want to risk anything of his own.
MacLaine plays Fran without self-pity or victimhood. Fran knows what’s happening to her — the compact mirror with the cracked glass that Baxter recognizes is the film’s most economical image: Fran has been seeing herself fractured and hasn’t stopped looking. Her suicide attempt isn’t melodramatic collapse; it’s the logical conclusion of a system that has used her completely. The Christmas Eve scene — Fran alone in Baxter’s apartment, the pills, the jazz record still playing — is Wilder at his most controlled: every element in the frame is doing work, and none of it is asking for your sympathy. It’s showing you what the system produces.
Baxter’s education
Baxter begins as a company man — literally giving up his private space for corporate advancement. His decency is real but passive: he’s kind to Fran, he feeds the neighbor’s cat, he doesn’t enjoy what’s happening in his apartment. But he allows it. The moral arc of the film is the movement from passive decency to active refusal.
The turning point is the key. Throughout the film, Baxter hands the key to his apartment to whoever asks. The act of handing over the key is the act of handing over himself — his space, his privacy, his agency. When he finally refuses Sheldrake (“I’ve decided to become a mensch”), he’s not making a grand gesture. He’s declining to hand over the key. The revolution is administrative. Wilder understands that in a corporate system, morality isn’t dramatic — it’s procedural. You don’t slay the dragon. You stop filling out the form.
Relation to Wilder
The Apartment is Wilder’s warmest film and his most architecturally precise. The screenplay (co-written with I.A.L. Diamond) is constructed like the insurance office: every element in its correct position, every scene advancing multiple lines simultaneously, the geometry producing meaning. It won the Academy Award for Best Picture, which means that in 1960, Hollywood gave its highest honor to a film about corporate sexual exploitation, a woman’s suicide attempt, and a man learning to stop being compliant — all disguised as a romantic comedy. Wilder would have appreciated the irony.
Connects to

Architecture as psychology in cinema — the office grid as suppression of individuality; the apartment as contested space between corporate power and private life; the elevator as liminal zone between floors and between selves
Modernist alienation — alienation not as existential condition but as institutional product; Baxter’s loneliness manufactured by the same system that promises community
Identity as performance — Baxter performing the company man; Fran performing the cheerful elevator girl; Sheldrake performing the concerned lover; every identity in the film shaped by institutional role
Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism — romantic comedy conventions used to tell a story about exploitation; the genre’s promise of happiness deployed alongside the evidence that happiness requires refusing the system the genre operates within
(1950) Sunset Boulevard — Wilder’s companion piece: both about people who sell their private space (mansion, apartment) to a system that consumes them; Joe is destroyed, Baxter escapes
(1961) La Notte — both films about alienation within modern architecture and institutions, though Antonioni’s alienation is spiritual and Wilder’s is structural

FILM/FILMS/(1961)-La-Notte.title: (1961) La Notte
FILM/FILMS/(1961)-La-Notte.content:
Director: Michelangelo Antonioni
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism
Giovanni, a successful novelist, and Lidia, his wife, spend a day and a night in Milan. They visit a dying friend in the hospital. They attend a book party for Giovanni’s latest novel. Lidia walks through the city alone. They go to a party at an industrialist’s villa that stretches through the night. By dawn, their marriage has been exposed as hollow — not through confrontation or crisis but through accumulation: small failures of attention, missed connections, the steady draining of feeling from gestures that once meant something. Nothing dramatic happens. Everything has already happened, offscreen, over years. The film is the record of two people discovering what they’ve already lost.
La Notte is the middle panel of Antonioni’s alienation trilogy, between (1960) L’Avventura and (1962) L’Eclisse. Where L’Avventura removes a person and watches the void fill with drift and betrayal, La Notte keeps both people present and watches the space between them widen until it becomes the film’s subject. No one disappears. No one leaves. That’s worse.
Key formal strategies

Single-day/night structure: dawn to dawn, real time compressing years of erosion into twenty-four hours
Milan’s architecture — hospital, office towers, construction sites, the villa — as a map of emotional states
Lidia’s walk: an extended sequence of urban wandering that replaces dialogue with spatial experience
The party as social architecture: the villa’s rooms, gardens, and pools organizing characters into patterns of desire and avoidance
Jeanne Moreau’s face and body language as primary text — what Lidia knows is carried physically, not spoken
Mastroianni’s Giovanni performing charm and intellect as habits that have outlived their content


The hospital
The film opens with Giovanni and Lidia visiting Tommaso, a writer and close friend, who is dying. The visit establishes everything: Giovanni performs warmth and concern, but the performance is visible — he’s already thinking about his book party, about himself. Lidia is quieter, more present, more devastated. She leaves the room in tears. Giovanni stays, receives a kiss from a young woman in the ward (a patient, possibly delusional), and doesn’t resist.
This opening is Antonioni’s thesis compressed into ten minutes. Giovanni’s inability to be fully present even at a dying friend’s bedside; his passive acceptance of the young woman’s advance; Lidia’s grief and her recognition that Giovanni’s grief is shallow — the marriage’s diagnosis is delivered before the film’s first act is over. Everything that follows is confirmation.
Lidia’s walk
After the book party, Lidia leaves and walks through Milan alone. This sequence — roughly fifteen minutes of a woman moving through urban space — is the film’s formal center. She walks past construction sites, through working-class neighborhoods, watches boys fighting, stands near a crumbling wall, finds herself at the edge of the city where the buildings thin out into empty lots.
Antonioni films this with the same attention he gives to the volcanic island in L’Avventura: the city is the subject as much as Lidia is. But where the island was hostile and barren, Milan is indifferent — busy with its own life, its construction and demolition, its ordinary violence. Lidia moves through it without destination. She’s not searching for anything. She’s experiencing, physically, the condition the marriage has produced: being alone while technically still accompanied, moving through space without purpose, feeling the texture of a world that continues whether or not you participate in it.
The walk is also Antonioni’s purest statement of architecture as psychology. The crumbling walls, the half-built towers, the empty lots — these aren’t metaphors for the marriage. They’re the spatial equivalent of what it feels like to be inside it: structures that were built for one purpose now serving another, or serving none.
The party
The industrialist Gherardini’s villa occupies the film’s second half. The party is a social machine: wealthy, cultured people circulating through designed spaces, performing connection. Giovanni flirts with Valentina (Monica Vitti), the host’s daughter — young, intelligent, attracted to him. Lidia watches, drifts, is pursued by a man she doesn’t want. The villa’s architecture — interior rooms, exterior gardens, the swimming pool — organizes the characters into geometries of desire and avoidance.
Antonioni uses the party the way he uses landscape: as an environment that reveals what the characters can’t articulate. Giovanni’s attraction to Valentina isn’t about Valentina — it’s about his need to be seen as vital, interesting, desired. His charm works on Valentina because she hasn’t yet learned what Lidia has: that Giovanni’s attention is a performance, brilliant on the surface, empty underneath. The party provides an audience, and Giovanni needs an audience the way the dying Tommaso needs oxygen.
Lidia, meanwhile, becomes increasingly visible — to us, if not to Giovanni. Moreau’s performance across these party scenes is extraordinary in its restraint. She doesn’t sulk or confront or weep. She simply is — present, watchful, aware. Her awareness is the film’s conscience, and it’s devastating because she has no use for it. Knowing what your marriage has become doesn’t repair it. Consciousness without agency: this is Antonioni’s definition of modern alienation.
The ending
Dawn. The party has emptied. Giovanni and Lidia walk onto the villa’s golf course. Lidia reads aloud a love letter — passionate, intimate, full of specific sensory memory. Giovanni doesn’t recognize it. She tells him he wrote it, years ago, to her.
The scene works because Antonioni has spent the entire film establishing Giovanni as a writer — a man whose profession is language, whose identity is built on the ability to articulate feeling. And here, confronted with his own words, he can’t connect them to himself. The letter is evidence of a person he no longer is, written to a woman he can no longer see. Language has failed at the deepest level: not communication between two people, but continuity within a single self.
They embrace on the grass. It isn’t reconciliation. It’s closer to what the ending of L’Avventura achieves — two people in a landscape, holding each other not out of love but out of the recognition that this is all there is. The gesture is real; the feeling behind it is uncertain; the dawn light is indifferent.
The dread
La Notte produces something that reads, experientially, like the dread in a David Lynch film — but through opposite means. Lynch generates dread through dream logic, uncanny doubling, the sense that the surface conceals something monstrous. Antonioni generates it through removal: the long takes, the open spaces, the quiet. Nothing is hidden. Nothing lurks. And that’s what’s frightening — the possibility that there is no depth beneath the surface, that the emptiness is all there is.
The formal overlap is real: both filmmakers use architecture to externalize psychic states, both hold shots past the point of narrative usefulness, both construct worlds that feel wrong without anything being visibly out of place. But where Lynch’s wrongness comes from surplus — too much darkness, too much sound, too much underneath — Antonioni’s comes from deficit. The villa is too large, the conversation too smooth, the party too long. The phoniness isn’t concealing something; it is the something. Giovanni’s charm, the guests’ sophistication, the designed spaces — these are performances that have replaced the feelings they once expressed. Dread, here, is the recognition that the performance is all that’s left.
Relation to Antonioni
La Notte is Antonioni’s most concentrated film — the trilogy’s chamber piece. L’Avventura has the island, the search, Sicily; L’Eclisse has the stock exchange, the empty streets, the formal coda. La Notte has two people and a city for one night. The compression makes it the most painful of the three: there’s no mystery to defer the reckoning, no formal abstraction to aestheticize the loss. Just a marriage, examined with clinical patience, until there’s nothing left to discover except what’s missing.
It’s also the film where Antonioni’s use of architecture reaches its most intimate scale. The hospital, the streets, the villa — these aren’t vast modernist voids but social spaces, designed for human use, and the gap between their design and how these particular humans use them is where the meaning lives.
Connects to

Architecture as psychology in cinema — Milan as emotional landscape: the hospital, the crumbling walls, the villa’s rooms organizing feeling the way blueprints organize space
Modernist alienation — alienation not as dramatic crisis but as the ordinary condition of a marriage that has used up its capacity for connection
Existential drift — Lidia’s walk as the trilogy’s purest instance of drift: movement without purpose through a city that doesn’t notice
Ellipsis and absence in modernist cinema — the marriage’s collapse happened before the film begins; what we see is the aftermath, the absence where love was
Movement-image vs time-image — the sensory-motor link dissolved: no action drives the narrative, only duration and observation
(1960) L’Avventura — companion piece: where L’Avventura watches a search become drift, La Notte watches a marriage become architecture
(1962) L’Eclisse — the trilogy’s final movement: from missing person to missing feeling to missing people

FILM/FILMS/(1961)-Last-Year-at-Marienbad.title: (1961) Last Year at Marienbad
FILM/FILMS/(1961)-Last-Year-at-Marienbad.content:
Original title: L’Année dernière à Marienbad
Director: Alain Resnais
Writer: Alain Robbe-Grillet
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Left Bank
A man approaches a woman at a baroque hotel and insists they met last year, perhaps here, perhaps at Marienbad. He says they had an affair. He says she promised to leave with him. She says she doesn’t remember. The film never confirms who’s telling the truth—or whether truth is the right category. For ninety minutes, Resnais and Robbe-Grillet loop through the same conversations, the same corridors, the same frozen poses, building a structure where memory, desire, and narration become indistinguishable.
Key formal strategies

Gliding tracking shots through symmetrical architecture
Repeated dialogue and gestures with variations
Organ score creating hypnotic atmosphere
Theatrical staging and frozen tableaux
Characters designated by letters (X, A, M) rather than names
Temporal loops that prevent chronological reading


The hotel
The setting is unnamed but unmistakable: an enormous baroque palace, all mirrors and chandeliers and geometric gardens. Resnais’s camera glides through corridors in long tracking shots, the voiceover describing the space with obsessive precision—the moldings, the paintings, the endless doors. No one seems to arrive or leave. The guests stand in frozen arrangements like figures in a painting.
The architecture functions as psychology externalized (see Architecture as psychology in cinema). The hotel is a labyrinth with no exit, a mind that can’t stop circling. Whether it’s X’s mind, A’s mind, or no one’s mind, the film doesn’t say.
The game
Throughout the film, M challenges other guests to a match game—rows of cards or matchsticks arranged in a specific pattern. The rules seem simple; M always wins. The game is called Nim, though the film never names it. X plays and loses. Everyone plays and loses.
It’s a perfect metaphor for the film itself: a structure with hidden rules that the characters (and viewers) can’t quite master. M’s control over the game parallels his control over A—or is it X who’s trying to control the narrative? The game recurs, each iteration slightly different, like everything else in the film.
The persuasion
X keeps approaching A with his story: they met, they talked in the gardens, she wore a white dress, she was afraid but ultimately willing. A resists, denies, seems frightened. X repeats the story with variations, adding details, insisting on his version. Is he remembering or inventing? Is he seducing or coercing?
Robbe-Grillet’s screenplay treats narration as an act of power. X isn’t just recounting the past; he’s trying to make it exist by sheer repetition. The ethical question hanging over the film is whether this constitutes memory, fantasy, or something closer to violation—overwriting someone else’s experience with your own.
The bedroom scene
Multiple times, the film shows (or imagines, or remembers) A in a white bedroom, arms spread, X approaching. Each version differs. In one she’s willing; in another she recoils. In one the room is bright; in another, shadowed. The repetition doesn’t clarify—it multiplies possibilities. Did this happen? Which version happened? The question dissolves into the structure.
This is Memory as structure in modernist cinema at its most radical. Memory isn’t retrieval; it’s generation. Each act of remembering produces a new version, and the film refuses to anchor any version as original.
The garden
The famous image: guests frozen in geometric arrangements on a terrace, their shadows falling at impossible angles while the conical trees cast no shadows at all. It’s a surrealist tableau, obviously artificial, obviously symbolic. Of what? The film doesn’t explain.
The garden represents the film’s method in miniature: beautiful, precise, and resistant to interpretation. You can assign meaning—death, timelessness, the unreality of memory—but the image exceeds any single reading. It’s a puzzle without a solution.
The ending
A finally agrees to leave with X. Or does she? They walk through the gardens at night, her voiceover uncertain, his narration continuing. The film ends mid-sentence, mid-gesture, with nothing resolved. If she’s leaving, we don’t see her leave. If she’s surrendering to his version of events, we don’t know what that means.
Resnais and Robbe-Grillet aren’t withholding a secret; they’re demonstrating that the secret doesn’t exist. The film is the question, not the answer.
Relation to Resnais
Marienbad is Resnais’s most formally extreme film—architecture as pure thought, narrative as pure pattern. It abandons the emotional urgency of Hiroshima for something colder and more abstract, but the underlying concern is the same: how memory constructs (and deconstructs) reality.
Connects to

Memory as structure in modernist cinema — memory here generates competing realities rather than recovering a single past
Architecture as psychology in cinema — the hotel as externalized mental space
Ellipsis and absence in modernist cinema — the central event (did they meet? did something happen?) is permanently withheld

FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Cléo-from-5-to-7.title: (1962) Cléo from 5 to 7
FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Cléo-from-5-to-7.content:
Original title: Cléo de 5 à 7
Director: Agnès Varda
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Left Bank
A pop singer waits ninety minutes to learn whether she has cancer. Varda structures the film in near real time, turning an ordinary Paris afternoon into something unbearable and transformative. What begins as a portrait of a woman who exists entirely through being looked at becomes, by the end, a study in how attention itself can shift—how the act of seeing outward, rather than performing for the gaze, changes everything.
Key formal strategies

Near real-time structure creating existential pressure
Mirrors and reflections fragmenting the protagonist’s self-image
Diegetic music that shifts from performance to confession
Gradual transition from interiors to street-level handheld shooting
Chapter titles marking time, making duration visible


Modernist problem
How do you film a woman’s interiority without turning her into a symbol or a spectacle?
Varda’s answer is duration. By locking us into Cléo’s time, forcing us to wait alongside her, the film makes her fear palpable rather than decorative. This anticipates later Feminist frameworks in cinema by decades, though Varda wasn’t working from theory—she was working from instinct and attention.
The mirror scenes
Cléo is introduced through reflections. The tarot reader’s table, shop windows, her own apartment—mirrors proliferate in the first half, and Varda shoots them so that Cléo appears fractured, multiplied, never quite whole. The famous moment where she tries on hats in a boutique (around 20 minutes in) is staged almost as a music video: she performs for herself, checking angles, adjusting expressions. The camera watches her watch herself. We’re implicated in the looking—a reflexive move that makes spectatorship itself visible.
Later, alone in her apartment after the songwriters leave, she tears off her wig in front of the mirror and the performance collapses. “Everyone spoils me,” she says. “No one loves me.” The line could be maudlin, but Varda holds the shot long enough that we feel the gap between the image and the person maintaining it.
The songs
Bob, the songwriter, plays her two new tracks. The first is frothy pop, and Cléo performs it dutifully, hitting her marks. Then he plays “Sans toi”—a song about death and absence—and something shifts. Varda holds on Cléo’s face as she sings, and the playful irony drains out of the scene. The lyrics are too close. This is one of the film’s pivots: entertainment becomes confession, and the distinction between Cléo-as-performer and Cléo-as-person briefly dissolves.
The scene also works as metacommentary. Varda is showing us how pop culture can accidentally tell the truth, how the machinery of image-making sometimes produces genuine feeling despite itself.
Paris as structure
The film divides into chapters, each timed and titled. This could feel gimmicky, but it does real work: it makes the city legible as a series of encounters rather than a backdrop. Cléo moves through distinct social textures—the superstitious intimacy of the tarot reading, the commercial space of the hat shop, the bourgeois discomfort of the café where men stare, the openness of the park. The chapter structure also creates a rhythm of ellipsis—we skip between locations, aware of what’s being compressed.
Movement matters. In the first half, Cléo is largely driven places or confined to interiors. After she leaves her apartment alone, the camera follows her into the street, and the visual grammar loosens. Handheld shots, ambient sound, faces in passing. She becomes one figure among many rather than the center of attention. The shift from interior to exterior tracks her psychological movement—space as emotional state.
The soldier
Antoine appears late, a chance encounter in Parc Montsouris. He’s on leave before returning to Algeria—facing his own mortality, though he treats it lightly. Their conversation is unguarded in a way nothing else in the film has been. He doesn’t flatter her or perform interest in her celebrity. She doesn’t pose.
What makes the scene work is its lack of romantic cliché. They talk, they take a bus, they walk. Varda doesn’t sentimentalize the connection or suggest it will save anyone. But something has shifted: Cléo is finally talking to someone rather than being looked at by them. The reciprocity is quiet and real.
The ending
The diagnosis scene refuses climax. Cléo and Antoine arrive at the hospital, she sees the doctor briefly, he confirms the illness but says it’s treatable. The conversation happens almost in passing, half-overheard. Then she and Antoine sit on a bench.
“I think I’m happy,” she says. It’s an odd line—hedged, uncertain. But it registers as true precisely because it’s so modest. The film doesn’t offer cure or transcendence. It offers something smaller: a woman who has stopped waiting to be seen and started, tentatively, to see.
Relation to Varda
This is Varda’s clearest statement of what feminist modernism might look like in practice. Duration, street photography, attention to surfaces—all the formal tools of the New Wave—are repurposed here to track a shift in consciousness. The experiment isn’t alienation for its own sake. It’s an attempt to show what it feels like to stop being an image.
Connects to

Existential drift — though Cléo’s wandering is structured by waiting and visibility rather than the aimless freedom of male New Wave protagonists

FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Il-sorpasso.title: (1962) Il sorpasso
FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Il-sorpasso.content:
Director: Dino Risi
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism (adjacent)
Mode: Commedia all’italiana
It’s Ferragosto, the August holiday when Rome empties. Roberto, a shy law student, is studying alone in his apartment when Bruno pulls up in his Lancia Aurelia convertible, honking, demanding a phone. Bruno is forty, loud, charming, restless—a man who survives on momentum. He drags Roberto out for what becomes a two-day road trip down the Italian coast: beaches, restaurants, old flames, family visits, constant motion. Roberto starts to loosen. Bruno starts to crack. The ending arrives without warning and changes everything that came before. Risi made a comedy that’s also a diagnosis—of the economic miracle, of Italian masculinity, of a culture accelerating toward a cliff it refuses to see.
Key formal strategies

Road-movie structure with episodic encounters
Location shooting on Roman streets and coastal highways
Fast-paced dialogue and improvisational energy
Tonal oscillation between comedy and sudden darkness
The car as spatial and psychological container
Abrupt ending that reframes everything retroactively


Bruno
Vittorio Gassman plays Bruno as pure velocity—talking, driving, flirting, lying, never stopping long enough for anything to stick. He’s seductive and exhausting. He knows everyone and belongs nowhere. His marriage failed. His daughter barely recognizes him. His business dealings are vague. But none of this registers as tragedy because Bruno won’t let it. He’s too busy being charming, too busy moving.
Risi lets us enjoy Bruno before he lets us see him. The performance is magnetic, and the film rides that magnetism for most of its runtime. Only gradually do the cracks appear: the boasts that don’t quite land, the pauses that last a beat too long, the family dinner where Bruno’s absence from his own life becomes visible. Drift here wears the mask of vitality.
Roberto
Jean-Louis Trintignant plays Roberto as Bruno’s opposite: cautious, bookish, repressed. He’s studying for his law degree while the rest of Rome vacations. He’s never had an adventure; he’s barely had a life. Bruno drags him into motion, and Roberto resists, then yields, then starts to enjoy himself.
The film’s warmth comes from watching Roberto open up—swimming, flirting, staying out all night. But Risi is careful not to frame this as liberation. Roberto isn’t becoming Bruno; he’s being borrowed by him, absorbed into someone else’s rhythm. The question the film poses without answering: is loosening up the same as living?
The road
The Aurelia convertible moves through a changing Italy: Roman suburbs, coastal resorts, small towns, highway construction. The economic miracle is visible everywhere—new cars, new buildings, new money—but also its anxieties. Traffic jams. Aggressive drivers. The “sorpasso” of the title—the dangerous overtake—is both driving technique and metaphor.
Speed here isn’t freedom; it’s compulsion. Bruno can’t slow down because slowing down would mean confronting what the motion conceals. The road promises transformation but delivers only circulation. Every destination becomes another departure.
The encounters
The episodic structure lets Risi build a portrait of Italian society through glancing contact. Bruno’s ex-wife, remarried and uncomfortable. His daughter, sullen and distant. An old flame who’s moved on. A German tourist couple. A funeral procession that Bruno honks through. Each encounter reveals something about Bruno—his failures, his loneliness, the relationships he’s abandoned—without the film ever stopping to moralize.
The comedy comes from Bruno’s ability to slide through every situation with charm and deflection. The melancholy comes from noticing what he’s deflecting.
The ending
On a cliff-side road, Bruno attempts another sorpasso. The car goes over. Roberto dies. Bruno survives.
The shot is held just long enough to register, then the film ends. No aftermath, no grief, no moral. Risi cuts the comedy off at the moment it becomes tragedy, refusing to process the death for us. Everything before—every joke, every charming scene—is suddenly reframed. The film’s pleasure becomes complicity. We were enjoying Bruno too, and look where it led.
Relation to Risi
Il sorpasso is Risi’s masterpiece and the defining work of commedia all’italiana: comedy as social diagnosis, laughter that curdles. The film operates simultaneously as entertainment and critique, never announcing its seriousness but embedding it in structure. Bruno is Italy’s economic miracle personified—all forward motion, all charm, all refusal to look at what’s being lost.
Connects to

Existential drift — motion without purpose, speed as avoidance
(1960) La dolce vita — companion diagnosis of early-sixties Italy, tragedy wearing glamour’s mask
Modernist alienation — but here the alienation is masked by performance, visible only in the gaps

FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Jules-et-Jim.title: (1962) Jules et Jim
FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Jules-et-Jim.content:
Director: François Truffaut
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
Two friends fall in love with the same woman. They share her, more or less, for twenty years—through bohemian Paris, World War I, Weimar Germany, and back to France. Then she drives them off a bridge. Jules et Jim is a film about romantic freedom that ends in catastrophe, made with such lightness and velocity that the darkness creeps up on you.
Key formal strategies

Rapid editing and temporal compression
Lyrical voiceover narration
Still photographs and archival footage integrated into fiction
Swirling camera movements that mirror emotional states
Tonal shifts from exuberance to melancholy


The race across the bridge
Early in the film, Catherine challenges Jules and Jim to a footrace across a footbridge. She cheats—starts early—and wins. Truffaut shoots it with handheld giddiness, the camera barely keeping up. It’s a throwaway moment, but it establishes Catherine’s terms: she makes the rules, and if you want to be near her, you play her game.
Jeanne Moreau plays Catherine as someone who runs on impulse and charm, who can make you feel like the center of her world until suddenly you’re not. The race is the whole film in miniature.
Catherine’s disguise
Catherine dresses as a man—Thomas—and the three of them walk through Paris. Jules and Jim are delighted; Truffaut lingers on Moreau’s face as she adopts a swagger, tests out masculine gestures. It’s play, but it’s also something else: Catherine refusing to be contained by the roles available to her.
The scene anticipates feminist readings of the film. Catherine’s restlessness isn’t pathology; it’s a response to a world that offers women limited scripts. She keeps inventing new ones, even when they destroy her.
The statue
Before meeting Catherine, Jules and Jim see a slide of an ancient stone sculpture—a woman’s face with an enigmatic smile. They’re transfixed. When they meet Catherine, her face matches the statue. The implication is clear: they’re in love with an image, a projection, and the real woman will never quite align with it.
This is Truffaut’s quiet thesis about romantic idealization. Jules and Jim don’t love Catherine; they love what they’ve decided she represents. The film watches what happens when a person refuses to stay frozen in someone else’s fantasy.
The war
World War I arrives in montage—archival footage, still photographs, Georges Delerue’s score shifting from playful to elegiac. Jules is Austrian; Jim is French. They fight on opposite sides, each terrified of killing the other.
The war divides the film in half. Before: youth, experimentation, the sense that life can be reinvented. After: consequences, compromise, the slow erosion of possibility. Truffaut doesn’t moralize about the war, but he lets its weight settle over everything that follows.
The chalet years
After the war, the three of them try to live together in a German chalet with Catherine’s daughter. It doesn’t work. Catherine cycles between Jules and Jim, restless and dissatisfied. Jim cycles back to his Parisian lover. Everyone is trying to sustain an arrangement that requires more generosity than any of them actually have.
These scenes are shot with a sadness that the characters don’t quite acknowledge. Truffaut’s camera observes their attempts at bohemian freedom while registering the exhaustion underneath. The dream of living outside convention turns out to require a kind of emotional labor none of them anticipated.
The ending
Catherine asks Jim to go for a drive. Jules watches from a distance. She accelerates onto a broken bridge and drives off the edge. The car sinks; both drown. Truffaut cuts to their cremation, their ashes mixed together in an urn.
The ending is shocking but not unmotivated. Catherine has always lived by impulse; this is her last one. What makes it tragic is how little catharsis it provides. Jules survives, attends the funeral, and the voiceover tells us he felt almost relieved. Twenty years of love, and what remains is exhaustion.
Connects to

Existential drift — but here drift is disguised as freedom; movement doesn’t lead anywhere stable
Ellipsis and absence in modernist cinema — rapid temporal jumps compress decades into montage, skipping over as much as they show

FILM/FILMS/(1962)-L'Eclisse.title: (1962) L'Eclisse
FILM/FILMS/(1962)-L'Eclisse.content:
Director: Michelangelo Antonioni
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism
Vittoria has just ended a relationship. She meets Piero, a stockbroker, at the Rome exchange where her mother gambles. They circle each other, almost connect, make plans to meet at a certain corner at a certain time. The film ends with seven minutes of that corner—streetlamps, sprinklers, a nurse pushing a pram, construction materials, faces we don’t recognize—and neither of them appears. Antonioni’s final installment in the alienation trilogy is his most radical: a film that gradually empties itself of its characters, replacing human drama with architectural space, ambient sound, and the texture of a world that continues whether anyone inhabits it or not.
Key formal strategies

Long takes and elliptical editing that refuse narrative momentum
Architecture and urban space as primary expressive material
Restrained performances—emotion withheld rather than displayed
Environmental sound replacing score
Seven-minute coda that removes protagonists entirely


The breakup
The film opens in medias res: Vittoria (Monica Vitti) and her boyfriend sit in his apartment, exhausted, the relationship already over. They barely speak. He adjusts objects. She stares out windows. Nothing is explained—no cause, no argument, no resolution. The scene runs nearly fifteen minutes, and when Vittoria finally leaves, we understand less about what happened than when we started.
This is alienation as method. Antonioni doesn’t dramatize the end of love; he films its aftermath as a series of silences, hesitations, and incomplete gestures. The apartment’s modernist architecture—clean lines, empty spaces, objects carefully arranged—becomes a diagram of emotional vacancy.
The stock exchange
Vittoria visits her mother at the Rome Borsa, where stocks are traded in a pit of shouting men. The sequence is extraordinary: chaos, noise, incomprehensible signals, money moving in patterns no individual controls. Piero (Alain Delon) works here, confident and efficient, perfectly adapted to the environment.
Antonioni films capitalism as ritual—abstract, impersonal, detached from any object or use. Value circulates; people respond; no one seems to understand the system they serve. When the market observes a minute of silence for a dead trader, then immediately resumes screaming, the point is made without commentary. Space here is not background but logic.
Vittoria and Piero
Their courtship, if it can be called that, consists of provisional gestures. They meet, talk, almost kiss. She’s drawn to him and also skeptical—he’s too fluent, too adjusted, too at home in a world she finds alienating. He’s attracted to her difference, her hesitation, her refusal to perform availability.
Antonioni shoots their scenes with careful distance. The camera observes rather than participates. Conversations drift into silence. Intimacy approaches but doesn’t arrive. Their relationship isn’t failing—it never consolidates enough to fail. It’s drift in real time.
The EUR
Several scenes take place in the EUR (Esposizione Universale Roma), the district Mussolini commissioned in the late 1930s for a world’s fair that never happened. The project was designed to showcase Italian rationalist architecture — a Fascist variant of international modernism that merged classical Roman forms with stripped-down geometric abstraction. The signature building, the Palazzo della Civiltà Italiana (the “Square Colosseum”), is a six-story grid of identical arches: Roman triumphal architecture reduced to pattern, emptied of ornament, scaled to intimidate. The district was meant to produce ideological submission — the individual dwarfed by the state’s monumental self-image.
By the time Antonioni films there in 1962, the ideology is gone but the architecture remains. The EUR has become a business district and residential neighborhood, its Fascist monuments repurposed as office buildings and museums. What was designed to project power now projects emptiness. The buildings are still monumental, still geometric, still depopulated — but the meaning they were built to carry has evaporated, and what’s left is pure form: beautiful, indifferent, autonomous.
Vittoria moves through these spaces like a figure in a de Chirico painting — dwarfed, out of scale, aesthetically absorbed into the architecture. Antonioni isn’t using the EUR as political commentary. He’s using it because these buildings already do what his cinema does: they produce alienation as a spatial experience. The wide boulevards with no pedestrians, the arcades that frame nothing, the surfaces that reflect light without warmth — the EUR becomes the film’s visual thesis. Architecture designed to make people feel small in the service of ideology now makes people feel small in the service of nothing. The buildings don’t care what they meant. They just stand there, and the humans moving through them are incidental.
The coda
Vittoria and Piero agree to meet at a familiar corner at eight o’clock. The scene shifts there—but they never arrive. Instead, Antonioni gives us seven minutes of the intersection: water flowing from a barrel, a bus stopping, passersby, a nurse with a baby carriage, construction scaffolding, the streetlight coming on, a close-up of a stranger’s face, the final image of the lamp filling the screen.
It’s one of cinema’s most radical endings. The characters we’ve followed have simply disappeared. The world continues—indifferent, textured, real. Whatever connection Vittoria and Piero might have made has been eclipsed. Not destroyed, not explained, just absent. The light still shines, but it doesn’t illuminate anything human.
Relation to Antonioni
L’Eclisse is Antonioni’s endpoint—the logical conclusion of the trilogy that began with L’Avventura and La Notte. Where those films kept their protagonists visible even as meaning dissolved, this one removes them entirely. The title’s metaphor—an eclipse, the blocking of light that doesn’t destroy the source—becomes literal. The world is still there. We’re just not in it anymore.
Connects to

Modernist alienation — alienation as structure, not theme
Architecture as psychology in cinema — the EUR as emotional diagram
Existential drift — relationships that never consolidate, time without destination
(1960) La dolce vita — both films map modern Rome as a space of disconnection

FILM/FILMS/(1962)-La-Jetée.title: (1962) La Jetée
FILM/FILMS/(1962)-La-Jetée.content:
Director: Chris Marker
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Left Bank
A man lies in a hammock underground, in Paris after World War III. Scientists send him back in time using only his memories. He keeps returning to the same image: a woman’s face on the observation pier at Orly, seen when he was a child. He also saw a man die there, but he doesn’t understand that yet. The film is twenty-eight minutes long, composed almost entirely of still photographs, and it may be the purest distillation of cinema’s relationship to memory and death ever made.
Key formal strategies

Narrative constructed from still photographs
Voiceover narration over static images
High-contrast black-and-white imagery
One brief moment of motion as emotional rupture
Circular structure where the ending is the beginning
Sound design that generates temporal flow against frozen images


The pier
The film begins and ends at Orly. A child watches planes with his parents. A woman stands on the observation pier. A man runs toward her, then falls. The child doesn’t understand what he’s seeing. He remembers the woman’s face—that image will haunt him for the rest of his life.
Marker presents this through still photographs, each held just long enough to register before cutting to the next. The rhythm is hypnotic: not quite cinema, not quite slideshow. We’re watching memory itself—frozen, fragmented, already past.
The experiments
After the war, survivors live underground. The surface is radioactive. Scientists need to send someone through time to find resources, but the journey destroys the mind. They discover that people with strong memories can survive. The protagonist has the strongest memory of all: that face on the pier.
The laboratory scenes are shot in harsh contrast—faces emerging from darkness, figures in hammocks, the protagonist blindfolded and injected. The voiceover describes the process clinically. He travels back, finds the woman, falls in love. He travels forward, finds a peaceful future. But the scientists don’t care about love or peace. They want power.
The woman
She appears in fragments across the film: her face in profile, her eyes closed, her hair against a pillow. The protagonist finds her in the past—at a museum, in a park, in bed. Their time together is rendered in still images that feel both intimate and impossible. He’s not really there. He’s remembering being there. Or he’s there, but as a ghost made of memory.
Marker never names her. She’s “the woman” throughout—an image before she’s a person. The film knows this is ethically troubling. To love a memory is to love something that can’t change, can’t resist, can’t be fully real.
The single moving shot
Two-thirds through the film, the woman opens her eyes. It’s the only moment of motion—maybe three seconds of footage. After twenty minutes of photographs, the movement is overwhelming. She blinks. She looks at him. Life breaks through the archive.
The effect is almost unbearable. Everything the film has been arguing about photographs and death—that still images freeze time, that they testify to what has already passed (see Photograph as death)—is momentarily reversed. Then it’s over, and we’re back to stills. The living moment is already gone.
The loop
The future people offer the protagonist asylum, but he asks to return to the past, to the woman, to Orly. He arrives on the pier. He sees her. He starts running toward her. Then he realizes: the scientists have followed him. The man he saw fall as a child was himself.
The structure closes into a circle. The memory that defined him was the memory of his own death. He couldn’t escape the past because the past is where he was always going. Trauma doesn’t repeat—it constitutes. You don’t have a past; the past has you.
Relation to Marker
La Jetée is Marker’s most concentrated work—essay film compressed into science fiction, philosophy rendered as fable. It establishes his central concerns: memory as structure, the archive as both preservation and entombment, the ethics of images that fix what should remain alive.
Connects to

Photograph as death — Barthes’s theory made cinematic: the photograph as evidence of “what has been,” mortality inscribed in every frozen image
Memory as structure in modernist cinema — memory here isn’t recalled but generative; it creates the narrative rather than supplementing it
(1959) Hiroshima mon amour — companion piece: where Resnais treats memory as fluid intrusion, Marker treats it as fixed fate

FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Vivre-sa-vie.title: (1962) Vivre sa vie
FILM/FILMS/(1962)-Vivre-sa-vie.content:
Original title: Vivre sa vie: Film en douze tableaux
Director: Jean-Luc Godard
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
Nana works in a record shop, wants to be an actress, falls into prostitution, and is killed in a deal gone wrong. Godard tells this story in twelve numbered chapters, each titled like an essay (“Nana meets Raoul” / “The Champs-Élysées”), mixing fiction with documentary technique. The result is something between character study and sociological inquiry—a film that watches a woman become an object while insisting on her interiority.
Key formal strategies

Twelve-chapter structure with intertitles
Mixture of fiction and documentary technique
Extended close-ups of the face
Back-of-head framing that obstructs access
Interpolated statistics and sociological data
Sound disjunctions and ambient noise


The opening shot
The first image is Nana from behind at a café counter, talking to a man we later learn is her estranged husband. Godard shoots the entire conversation from the back of her head—we see her neck, her hair, the mirror reflecting fragments of her face, but never a clean frontal view. It’s a choice that announces the film’s central tension: Nana is the subject, but the camera’s access to her is partial, obstructed.
Later, Godard will give us extended close-ups of Karina’s face, some of the most beautiful in cinema. But he starts by denying us, by marking the gap between a person and the images we make of her.
The Dreyer scene
Nana goes to the movies. The film is Dreyer’s The Passion of Joan of Arc. Godard cuts between Falconetti’s face on screen and Karina’s face in the audience, both in close-up, both in tears. It’s a moment of almost unbearable identification—two women, two faces, two images of suffering aestheticized by cinema.
The scene is doing double work. It’s genuinely moving, Nana touched by Joan’s martyrdom. But it’s also a question: what does it mean to watch a woman suffer beautifully? Godard is implicating himself and us. Cinema has always loved the female face in anguish. Is he escaping that tradition or extending it? This self-interrogation is part of the film’s reflexive method—and anticipates later feminist critiques of the male gaze.
The pimp
Raoul appears around the midpoint, handsome and businesslike. He explains the economics of prostitution with the detachment of a manager—prices, locations, client expectations. Nana listens. She doesn’t resist or collapse; she assesses. The transaction is shot matter-of-factly, like a job interview.
Godard interpolates statistics about Parisian prostitution, documentary-style. The effect is to situate Nana within a system: not a fallen woman, not a moral parable, but a worker entering an industry with its own regulations and hierarchies. The film refuses the melodrama that would make her exceptional.
The Brice Parain conversation
In the café scene with the philosopher Brice Parain, Nana asks real questions: What is language? How do we say what we mean? Can thought exist without words? Parain answers seriously, citing Plato, discussing the gap between conception and expression.
It’s an extraordinary sequence—a prostitute and a philosopher having an intellectual conversation in a Parisian café, filmed like a documentary interview. Nana is curious, engaged, following the argument. The scene insists on her interiority at exactly the moment when her profession reduces her to a body for sale. This is Language and form as thought made literal: philosophy staged as dialogue, thinking as dramatic action.
The tableaux structure
Each chapter is introduced with an intertitle describing its contents, like silent cinema or Brecht. The structure fragments narrative continuity—we jump across time, skip explanations, enter scenes mid-action. It’s a refusal of the smooth psychological arc that would make Nana’s decline feel inevitable or natural (see Ellipsis and absence in modernist cinema).
Instead, each tableau feels discrete, a unit of observation. Godard is studying Nana the way an anthropologist might, recording behaviors and contexts without synthesizing them into a story about individual choice or moral failure.
The ending
Nana is shot in a botched exchange between pimps. It happens quickly, almost casually—gunfire, bodies, silence. Godard doesn’t prepare us for it or give it emotional weight. She falls, the men drive away, the film ends.
The flatness is the point. The system that turned Nana into a commodity disposes of her like one. There’s no tragedy in the classical sense, no recognition or reversal. Just termination.
Connects to

Existential drift — Nana moves through the city without stable ground or clear direction

FILM/FILMS/(1963)-8½.title: (1963) 8½
FILM/FILMS/(1963)-8½.content:
Original title: Otto e mezzo
Director: Federico Fellini
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism
Guido Anselmi is a famous director who cannot make his next film. He retreats to a spa, pursued by producers, journalists, his wife, his mistress, and the ghosts of his childhood. He has no script, no vision, only a massive rocket-ship set being built for a movie that may never exist. The film we’re watching is the film he can’t make—or maybe it’s what happens inside his head while he fails to make it. Fellini abandons linear storytelling entirely, moving through memory, fantasy, and present-tense chaos with no hierarchy between them. Creative paralysis becomes the engine rather than the obstacle. The result is cinema’s most celebrated film about not being able to make a film.
Key formal strategies

Dream logic replacing causal narrative—past, present, and fantasy without transition
Baroque black-and-white cinematography with deep staging and ensemble movement
Recursive structure where the unmade film generates the film we watch
Women as projections of the protagonist’s psyche rather than autonomous characters
Circus and carnival imagery as metaphors for artistic creation


The opening
Guido is trapped in his car in a traffic jam, suffocating. He floats out the window, rises above the stalled vehicles, drifts over the ocean—then is yanked back to earth by a rope around his ankle, pulled down by men on a beach. Cut to: waking up. It’s a dream. Or it’s the film announcing its terms: this will be a movie about wanting to escape and being pulled back, about flight and gravity, about the gap between aspiration and constraint.
The sequence establishes that memory and fantasy will carry equal weight to waking life. There are no markers distinguishing one from another. Fellini trusts us to float.
The spa
Guido has fled Rome for a health resort, ostensibly to rest, actually to hide. The producer wants answers. The screenwriter wants to discuss the script’s intellectual framework. The set is being built. Everyone orbits Guido, demanding decisions he cannot make.
Fellini stages the spa as a theater of anxiety—long tracking shots through crowds, faces pressing in, conversations overlapping. Guido moves through it all with a frozen smile, deflecting, deferring, inventing lies. His creative block isn’t dramatized as anguish; it’s dramatized as social comedy, the absurdity of a man who has nothing to say being asked to say everything.
The harem fantasy
Guido imagines all the women in his life—wife, mistress, mother, childhood crushes, fantasy figures—gathered in the farmhouse of his youth, attending to him, competing for his attention. He cracks a whip. They adore him. Then they revolt.
The sequence is outrageous, self-indicting, and formally crucial. Fellini makes explicit what the whole film implies: Guido’s women are projections, fragments of his psyche rather than people. The fantasy isn’t hidden; it’s performed, examined, shown to be both seductive and pathetic. The face here is multiplied, fetishized, and finally emptied of autonomy.
Childhood and the Church
Memory intrudes without invitation. Young Guido watches the Saraghina, a heavy woman on the beach, dance for coins. The priests catch him, shame him, parade him before his family. Catholic guilt and erotic awakening fuse into a single complex that the adult Guido still hasn’t untangled.
These sequences aren’t flashbacks in the conventional sense—they don’t explain the present, they coexist with it. The past is as vivid and present as the spa, the set, the mistress’s hotel room. Time in 8½ is vertical, not horizontal. Everything happens at once.
The press conference
Near the end, Guido faces a press conference at his unfinished set. Journalists hurl questions. He has no answers. He crawls under the table. He pulls out a gun and shoots himself.
Except he doesn’t. The suicide is fantasy, like everything else. Guido emerges, and the film pivots into its final movement: acceptance. He’ll never make the film. The set will be torn down. But something else is possible.
The circus
Everyone appears—every character from every timeline, memory, and fantasy—and begins to circle the scaffolding of the abandoned set. A band plays. Young Guido leads them. Older Guido watches, then joins. The film ends with a spotlight narrowing on a small boy playing a flute in darkness.
It’s not resolution. The movie-within-the-movie remains unmade. But Fellini offers something else: the circus as metaphor for creation, chaotic, communal, and without destination. Art isn’t mastery. It’s surrender to the procession. Drift here is not despair but release.
Relation to Fellini
8½ is Fellini’s autobiography mythologized—not confession but transformation. The title refers to the number of films he’d made (six features, two short segments, one collaboration counted as half). The self-portrait is fractured, theatrical, and fully aware of its own narcissism. Fellini proves that creative crisis can generate form rather than prevent it.
Connects to

Memory as structure in modernist cinema — the past as lived present, not explained backstory
Existential drift — Guido’s paralysis as both condition and subject
(1960) La dolce vita — companion piece: where Marcello drifts through society, Guido drifts through his own psyche

FILM/FILMS/(1963)-Contempt.title: (1963) Contempt
FILM/FILMS/(1963)-Contempt.content:
Original title: Le Mépris
Director: Jean-Luc Godard
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
A screenwriter’s marriage disintegrates while he works on a doomed adaptation of The Odyssey. The producer is a boor. The director is Fritz Lang, playing himself. The wife is Brigitte Bardot, and the film opens with her nude body sliding across the screen in red, white, and blue light while she asks her husband to catalogue which parts of her he loves. It’s Godard’s most gorgeous film and his coldest—a movie about the death of feeling made with overwhelming visual beauty.
Key formal strategies

CinemaScope framing that emphasizes lateral distance between figures
Long tracking shots through architectural space
Color as ideological and emotional system
Film-within-a-film reflexivity
Withholding psychological explanation for emotional states


The apartment scene
The heart of Contempt is a thirty-minute sequence in Paul and Camille’s Rome apartment, shot in long lateral tracking movements as the couple drift through rooms, passing each other, circling back. Paul has just let the vulgar producer drive Camille home alone—a small humiliation that Camille registers and Paul refuses to acknowledge.
Nothing dramatic happens. They discuss a lamp. She takes a bath. He reads. She puts on a dark wig and asks if he still loves her. The camera follows them with geometric precision, mapping the space between them. By the end of the scene, something has broken, though neither character names it and no event explains it.
This is Godard doing Antonioni: alienation rendered through architecture, duration, and the failure of speech to reach anyone. The modernist apartment becomes a maze where intimacy goes to die.
Color as system
The film’s palette is strictly controlled—saturated reds, blues, and yellows that function as emotional and ideological markers rather than naturalistic light. Camille’s red bathrobe, the blue of the sea at Capri, the yellow of Prokosch’s sports car. These colors don’t express feeling; they structure it, creating a visual grammar of distance and power (see Color as meaning in modernist cinema).
When Camille puts on the black wig, she’s trying on a different version of herself—testing whether Paul’s desire is for her or for an image. The film keeps asking this question without answering it: what is the relationship between a woman and her appearance, between a person and the images that circulate in her place?
The Villa Malaparte
The final act takes place at the famous cliffside house on Capri, a fascist-era architectural statement jutting over the Mediterranean. Godard shoots it as a monument to inhuman beauty—geometric, isolated, impossible to inhabit warmly. Paul and Camille’s marriage ends here, in a space that makes human scale feel irrelevant.
The villa functions the way architecture always functions in modernist cinema: as psychology externalized. The building doesn’t represent alienation; it produces it. You can’t imagine anyone being happy in those rooms.
Lang and the Odyssey
Fritz Lang plays “Fritz Lang,” hired to direct an adaptation of Homer that the producer wants to turn into spectacle. Lang resists. Paul, the screenwriter, is caught between them—compromising his artistic conscience for money, telling himself it doesn’t matter.
The film-within-the-film doubles the film itself. Godard, too, is making a commercial production (financed by Carlo Ponti, starring Bardot) while trying to smuggle in his own concerns. The Odyssey footage we see is beautiful but fragmentary—statues of gods, the sea, Ulysses gazing at the horizon. It suggests an older mode of storytelling that the modern world has lost access to. The reflexive structure implicates Godard in the very compromises Paul makes.
Camille’s silence
Bardot’s performance is built around what she withholds. Camille stops explaining herself partway through the film. When Paul asks why she despises him, she says, “I despise you.” When he asks what he did, she says, “Nothing.” The contempt is there, fully present, but its origin remains obscure.
This is the film’s central modernist move: emotion without psychological explanation. We see the breakdown; we don’t get the backstory that would make it legible. Contempt arrives like weather.
The ending
Camille leaves with Prokosch. Their car crashes offscreen into a truck. Godard cuts to the wreckage—bodies, twisted metal, no buildup, no dramatic preparation. Paul learns of it almost in passing. The film ends with Lang filming the Odyssey scene, Ulysses finally seeing Ithaca on the horizon. “Silence,” Lang calls out.
It’s a conclusion that refuses catharsis. The death feels arbitrary, almost administrative. What remains is the work—Lang’s film continuing, indifferent to the human wreckage that produced it.

FILM/FILMS/(1963)-High-and-Low.title: (1963) High and Low
FILM/FILMS/(1963)-High-and-Low.content:
Original title: Tengoku to Jigoku (Heaven and Hell)
Director: Akira Kurosawa
Movement: Japanese Golden Age Cinema
Kingo Gondo is a wealthy shoe manufacturer on the verge of a corporate power play. He’s leveraged everything — his house, his savings — to buy controlling shares of his company. Then the phone rings: a kidnapper has taken his chauffeur’s son, mistaking him for Gondo’s own child. The ransom will bankrupt Gondo. The kidnapper realizes his mistake — and doesn’t care. Pay or the boy dies. Gondo must choose: his fortune or a child who isn’t his.
The title gives the structure away: heaven and hell, the hilltop mansion and the city below. Kurosawa splits the film into two radically different halves — the first a claustrophobic chamber drama in Gondo’s living room, the second a procedural manhunt through Yokohama’s underworld — and uses the split to map the vertical structure of a society where wealth literally sits above poverty, looking down.
Key formal strategies

The two-half structure: chamber drama (the house) → police procedural (the city)
Widescreen composition used to stage social hierarchy spatially — Gondo’s house on the hill, the kidnapper’s room looking up at it
The first half confined almost entirely to one room, with blocking and framing creating visual arguments about power and obligation
The shift from black and white to a single burst of color (pink smoke from the ransom bag) — the only color in the film
The final scene’s mirror: Gondo and the kidnapper facing each other through glass, each reflected in the other


The house
The first hour takes place almost entirely in Gondo’s living room — a space that Kurosawa transforms into a moral arena through blocking and composition alone. Characters are arranged in the wide frame according to their relationship to the dilemma: Gondo by the windows (the view down to the city), the police against the walls, Gondo’s wife moving between positions. The room that represents Gondo’s achievement — the hilltop house, the picture windows, the space — becomes his prison. Every compositional choice reinforces the central question: what does a man owe when the cost of doing right is everything he has?
This is Architecture as psychology in cinema operating through social rather than psychic logic. The house isn’t an expression of Gondo’s inner state (the Lynch mode); it’s a materialization of his class position. The windows that frame his view of the city below are the same windows through which the kidnapper, down in the slums, looks up and sees paradise.
The ransom decision
Gondo pays. The film doesn’t treat this as a heroic climax — it’s a quiet, exhausted capitulation. He pays because not paying is unthinkable, even though the child isn’t his, even though it will destroy him. The decision is moral in the most demanding sense: it costs everything and impresses no one. The police are sympathetic but can’t help him recover his fortune. His business partners proceed with their takeover. The act of moral courage is also the act that breaks him.
The procedural
The second half explodes outward — from one room to the entire city. The police investigation unfolds with meticulous, almost documentary precision: forensic analysis, stakeouts, foot pursues through alleys and jazz clubs and drug dens. Kurosawa tracks the investigation systematically, following the kidnapper’s trail downward through Yokohama’s social strata. The visual language shifts from theatrical staging (the house) to kinetic movement (the city). The two halves are compositionally and rhythmically opposite, which is the point: heaven is static, confined, suffocating in its privilege; hell is dynamic, sprawling, alive with desperate energy.
The pink smoke
In a black-and-white film, one image arrives in color: pink smoke rising from the ransom bag, marking the kidnapper’s location. It’s a technical flourish (audiences in 1963 would have gasped) but also a formal argument. The single color in a monochrome world marks the point where Gondo’s sacrifice becomes visible — where the money he gave up to save a child is literally burning. The smoke rises from the underworld toward the hilltop. Heaven and hell, connected by the pink plume of Gondo’s destroyed fortune.
The mirror
The final scene: Gondo visits the kidnapper on death row. They face each other through the glass partition. The kidnapper, a medical intern who lived in a sweltering room with a view of Gondo’s cool, lit mansion, explains his hatred — not for Gondo personally but for the arrangement that put paradise in his sightline while condemning him to hell. As they talk, the glass reflects their faces simultaneously: each man sees himself and the other, superimposed.
Kurosawa holds this image. The doubling — rich man and poor man, moral actor and amoral actor, heaven and hell — is made visually literal. Neither man is simplified. Gondo’s goodness didn’t save him from ruin; the kidnapper’s hatred doesn’t reduce to individual evil. The structure that produced both of them persists beyond the film.
Relation to Kurosawa
High and Low is Kurosawa’s most precisely constructed social analysis — the film where his humanist commitments and his formal ambition converge most tightly. The two-half structure (confinement/expansion, stasis/movement) anticipates techniques that other directors would explore decades later. The film’s refusal to let moral action produce narrative reward — Gondo does the right thing and is destroyed for it — is characteristically Kurosawan: his heroes earn respect, not victory.
Connects to

Architecture as psychology in cinema — the hilltop house as materialized class position; the kidnapper’s room as its inverse. Space as social structure, not psychic expression.
Identity as performance — the kidnapper and Gondo as doubles produced by social position rather than psychic fracture. The mirror shot literalizes the interchangeability.
Movement-image vs time-image — the two-half structure splits the distinction: the first half approaches time-image (theatrical staging, moral deliberation in real time), the second half is pure movement-image (pursuit, investigation, kinetic editing)
Epistemological doubt — the film doesn’t doubt whether truth is knowable. It doubts whether moral action can survive structural inequality.

FILM/FILMS/(1963)-The-Birds.title: (1963) The Birds
FILM/FILMS/(1963)-The-Birds.content:
Director: Alfred Hitchcock
Movement: Classical Hollywood cinema
Melanie Daniels, a wealthy San Francisco socialite, follows Mitch Brenner to the small coastal town of Bodega Bay to deliver a pair of lovebirds. The birds attack. First one gull, then flocks, then all species — coordinated, relentless, without explanation. The attacks escalate. People die. The town boards its windows. Melanie is trapped in an attic and nearly killed. The film ends with Mitch driving his family and Melanie through a landscape covered in birds, silent, waiting. No explanation is offered. No resolution arrives.
The Birds is Hitchcock’s most radical film — the one that comes closest to breaking with classical cinema entirely. There is no mystery to solve, no criminal to catch, no psychological explanation for the attacks. The birds simply come. The narrative structure of the first thirty minutes (a romantic comedy about Melanie and Mitch) is dismantled by a force that doesn’t obey narrative logic. Hitchcock subjects his own system — suspense, causality, resolution — to an assault as senseless as the birds themselves.
Key formal strategies

No musical score: replaced entirely by electronic bird sounds (designed by Oskar Sala and Remi Gassmann), stripping the audience of emotional guidance
The slow build: the first attack is a single gull; the escalation is gradual, each attack larger and less explicable
The absence of explanation: no scientist arrives, no cause is identified, the film refuses to solve itself
The schoolhouse sequence: children singing while crows gather silently behind Melanie — suspense as pure image
The ending: no resolution, no victory, no restoration of order — the family drives through a world that has permanently changed


The schoolhouse
Melanie sits outside the schoolhouse, smoking, while children sing inside. Behind her, on the jungle gym, crows land — one, then another, then dozens. She doesn’t see them. The camera cuts between her face and the gathering birds. The children’s song continues. No score. The tension is produced entirely by the gap between what Melanie knows and what the audience sees: the Hitchcockian bomb under the table, but this time the bomb is alive and multiplying.
This sequence is Hitchcock’s purest suspense — no dialogue, no movement, no violence, just the slow accumulation of threat in a quiet space. The singing children make it worse: innocence and menace occupying the same frame, neither aware of the other.
No explanation
Classical cinema explains. Something causes the problem; something resolves it. The Birds refuses both. The attacks begin without cause and end without resolution. Characters speculate — punishment from God, ecological disruption, Melanie herself as catalyst — but the film endorses none of these readings. The ornithologist at the diner insists birds couldn’t organize attacks. She’s wrong. Nobody is right.
The refusal to explain is the film’s most modernist gesture. It breaks the classical contract as thoroughly as anything in Antonioni or Resnais — not through formal experimentation but through the withdrawal of meaning. The world becomes hostile, and the hostility has no source, no logic, no end. This is Epistemological doubt operating inside a genre film: the mystery that refuses to be a mystery because there is nothing to know.
The ending
The family leaves. The car moves slowly through a landscape blanketed with birds — thousands of them, silent, still, watching. The car disappears. No “The End” title card. No score. No resolution.
Hitchcock said the birds represented “complacency” — the comfortable assumption that the natural world is under control. But the film is more unsettling than any allegory allows. The ending doesn’t mean something; it withholds meaning. The birds are there. They’re not leaving. The classical narrative has stopped, and what replaces it is the image of a world that no longer operates by human rules.
Relation to Hitchcock
The Birds is Hitchcock’s most anomalous film — the one where his classical methods encounter a subject that defeats them. Suspense works when you know the danger and understand its logic. The birds provide the danger but withhold the logic. The result is something closer to existential dread than thriller tension: a Hitchcock film that, in its final act, stops being a Hitchcock film and becomes something the vault doesn’t quite have a name for.
Connects to

Epistemological doubt — the refusal to explain the attacks; knowledge systems (science, religion, folk wisdom) all fail; the film withdraws meaning from its own genre
Active spectatorship in modernist cinema — the schoolhouse sequence activates the spectator through the gap between character knowledge and audience knowledge; the ending demands the spectator supply the meaning the film withholds
Architecture as psychology in cinema — Bodega Bay’s houses boarded against the attacks; domestic space as fortress that fails; the attic assault as architecture turned hostile
(1960) Psycho — both films kill or nearly kill the audience’s identification figure in a confined space (shower/attic); both use architecture as the site of bodily assault

FILM/FILMS/(1964)-A-Hard-Day's-Night.title: (1964) A Hard Day's Night
FILM/FILMS/(1964)-A-Hard-Day's-Night.content:
Director: Richard Lester
Influence: French New Wave
Four musicians are chased by fans, ride a train, evade their manager, wander off, get into trouble, play a concert, and escape again. That’s the plot, and it barely matters. What matters is the way Lester films it: handheld cameras, jump cuts, location shooting in actual London streets, direct address, non-sequitur dialogue, and a fluidity between documentary and fiction that could have come straight from the Cahiers screening room. A Hard Day’s Night is a commercial comedy starring the biggest pop group in the world, and it is also, unmistakably, a French New Wave film made in English — the most successful translation of Godard’s formal innovations into popular cinema, and the template for everything from music video to mockumentary.
Key formal strategies

Handheld camera and location shooting throughout London
Jump cuts and discontinuous editing directly inherited from Godard
Fourth-wall breaks and direct address to camera
Semi-improvised, absurdist dialogue closer to theater of the absurd than Hollywood comedy
Musical sequences as formal liberation — movement, cutting, and rhythm freed from narrative logic


The New Wave in English
Lester had absorbed the French New Wave completely. The evidence is everywhere: the handheld tracking shots through streets, the jump-cut editing that breaks continuity for energy and wit, the mixing of staged scenes with documentary-style observation, the actors looking into the camera and addressing the audience. The opening chase — the Beatles sprinting from fans through London streets, the camera pursuing them, the editing jagged and breathless — is (1960) Breathless transposed into comedy. Michel running through Paris, the Beatles running through London: both sequences use the city as playground, the camera as accomplice, and editing as rhythm rather than continuity.
But Lester isn’t simply copying Godard. He’s translating New Wave techniques for an audience that has never seen a Godard film and doesn’t need to. The jump cuts feel like excitement rather than formal disruption. The direct address feels like charm rather than alienation. The documentary-fiction blend feels like spontaneity rather than epistemological experiment. Lester understood that the New Wave’s innovations could do different work in a different context — that the same formal tools Godard used to dismantle cinema could be used to make cinema feel more alive, more present, more fun. This is the New Wave democratized: not cinema interrogating itself, but cinema vibrating at the frequency of its moment.
The train
The first extended sequence places the Beatles on a train with Paul’s fictional grandfather (Wilfrid Brambell), and the comedy plays almost entirely through dialogue — rapid, absurdist, full of non-sequiturs that resist logic without becoming surreal. When a stuffy businessman tells them to be quiet, the exchanges that follow have the rhythm of Ionesco: language as play, meaning as secondary to energy. The Beatles aren’t acting in the Hollywood sense; they’re being variations of themselves, and the semi-improvised quality (Lester encouraged ad-libbing, shot with multiple cameras to catch spontaneous moments) produces the same liveness that the New Wave achieved through similar methods.
